original_abstract:
This paper investigates the challenge of channel estimation in multi-cell interference-limited networks, specifically focusing on the "pilot contamination" effect in both finite and large-scale MIMO systems. Traditional estimation methods struggle to distinguish between desired signals and interference when pilot sequences are reused across cells, creating a performance bottleneck that does not vanish even with an infinite number of antennas.

We propose a novel, coordinated estimation framework that leverages the second-order statistical information (covariance) of user channels. By exploiting the spatial structure of the channel—specifically the finite angle-of-arrival (AOA) spread—we demonstrate that base stations can effectively discriminate between desired and interfering users even when their pilot sequences are identical. Analytically, we prove that if the signal subspaces of the desired and interfering channel covariances are non-overlapping, the pilot contamination effect vanishes completely as the number of antennas grows. 

To operationalize these findings, we introduce a low-rate coordination protocol using a covariance-aware pilot assignment strategy. This approach selectively groups users to maximize the spatial orthogonality of their channel statistics. Numerical simulations confirm that our method provides significant performance gains in terms of both estimation accuracy and achievable downlink rates, outperforming conventional frameworks even with moderate antenna array sizes and in scenarios with imperfect spatial separation.
humanized_abstract:
Channel estimation for multi-cell interference-limited networks is studied in this paper with a focus on the pilot contamination issue for both finite and large-scale MIMO systems. It is known that conventional channel estimation methods fail to differentiate between the desired and interfering signals due to the contamination of pilot signals when the same pilots are reused across different cells. As a result, this pilot contamination issue presents a fundamental performance barrier in multi-cell massive MIMO systems, and it does not diminish even when the number of antennas grows infinitely large.

We introduce a new approach to coordinated estimation based on the second order statistics of user channels, specifically their covariance. We show that using the structure of the channel, given by the fact that the angle-of-arrival (AOA) spread is finite, base stations can distinguish between useful and interfering signals even when they have the same pilot sequence. We prove that if the signal spaces of the channel covariances are disjoint, the pilot contamination effect reduces to zero in the large number of antennas regime.

Based on the conclusions in the previous section, we develop a low-rate coordination protocol based on a covariance-aware pilot assignment scheme. By wisely grouping users, such that the spatial orthogonality of their respective channel statistics is maximized, the corresponding CSI can be obtained with improved accuracy. The corresponding performance benefits in terms of CSI estimation accuracy and downlink achievable rates are demonstrated through simulations. It is shown that the proposed scheme can yield a substantial performance gain as compared with state-of-the-art schemes, even with moderate number of elements at the BS, and with some degree of element spacing.

document.output:
Channel estimation for multi-cell interference-limited networks is studied in this paper with a focus on the pilot contamination issue for both finite and large-scale MIMO systems. It is known that conventional channel estimation methods fail to differentiate between the desired and interfering signals due to the contamination of pilot signals when the same pilots are reused across different cells. We introduce a new approach to coordinated estimation based on the second order statistics of user channels, specifically their covariance. document.input:
This paper investigates the challenge of channel estimation in multi-cell interference-limited networks, specifically focusing on the "pilot contamination" effect in both finite and large-scale MIMO systems.
